1: The fellowship offering (shelamim, also rendered 'peace offering' or 'well-being offering') is the only offering in which the offerer participates in the meal alongside the priest. This communal dimension distinguishes it from the burnt offering (entirely to YHWH) and makes it the closest OT analogue to a covenant meal. The shelamim is derived from shalom (completeness, peace, well-being) — it is an offering of a person who is at peace with YHWH and wishes to celebrate or maintain that relationship. Male or female animals from the herd are acceptable (contrast the burnt offering's male-only requirement).
2: The offerer performs the slaughter at the sanctuary entrance — same as the burnt offering. The fellowship offering was often a celebratory sacrifice: celebrating answered prayer, completing a vow, or expressing spontaneous gratitude. Its communal nature (offerer eats a portion) made it the occasion for communal feasts. 1 Samuel 1:3-4 shows Elkanah distributing portions of the shelamim to his family at Shiloh — the offering was the occasion for the family gathering and meal.
3: The portions belonging to YHWH from the fellowship offering: (1) the fat covering the entrails (ḥelev hamekassah et-haqerev), (2) all the fat on the entrails, (3) the two kidneys with their fat. These internal fat deposits are the richest, most energy-dense parts of the animal. In ANE thought, the fat was the vitality of the creature and therefore the most appropriate gift to the deity. The kidneys were associated with the seat of deepest inner life and decision-making (often rendered 'heart' or 'reins' in KJV).
4: The fatty lobe of the liver (yoteret al-hakkaved) was significant in Mesopotamian divination (haruspicy) — liver examination was a major form of divination in Babylon and Assyria. Clay liver models from Mesopotamia have been found used for teaching divination techniques. Israel's law removes the liver from divinatory use by assigning the fatty lobe to YHWH — it is burned on the altar rather than examined for omens. This may be a deliberate polemical exclusion of divination practices.
5: The fat portions are burned by the priests on top of the burnt offering wood — the fellowship offering's fat shares the altar's fire with the burnt offering. The layering of offerings on the altar reflects a complex sacrificial calendar: multiple types of offerings could be in progress simultaneously, with the fellowship offering's fat additions placed on the already-burning burnt offering wood.
6: The flock version of the fellowship offering follows the same rules as the herd version — male or female, without defect. The consistency of the defect-free requirement (tamim) across all offering types reflects the principle that what is offered to YHWH must represent the best of what one has, not the culled or imperfect animals. Malachi 1:8 later condemns the practice of offering blind, lame, or sick animals — Leviticus establishes the standard that Malachi upholds.
7: The lamb as fellowship offering: the same procedure, with the added rule that the fat tail (ha'alyah) of certain sheep breeds is included in YHWH's portion. The fat tail refers to the large fat deposit on the tail of the broad-tailed sheep (Ovis aries), common in the Levant — these tails can weigh several kilograms and represent a significant fat deposit. This is a breed-specific addition to the standard fat portions.
8: The hand-laying and slaughter before the tent of meeting — consistent with the general offering protocol. The fellowship offering's blood-sprinkling against the altar maintains the blood-manipulation at the sacred space that defines all Levitical sacrifice.
9: The fat tail removed close to the backbone is added to YHWH's portion in the lamb offering. The careful specification of where the tail is cut (leummat he'atset, close to the backbone) reflects the precision with which even anatomical instructions are given in Leviticus. Nothing about the offering is left to improvisation.
10: The two kidneys and their fat from the lamb — same as the cattle and flock rules. The standardization of YHWH's portions across all three animal categories (cattle, sheep, goat) ensures consistency regardless of species. The law is comprehensive and species-independent in its logic.
11: lehem isheh laYHWH: 'food of a food offering to YHWH.' The fellowship offering's fat portions are described as 'food' (lehem, bread/food) offered by fire (isheh, fire-offering). This language of feeding the deity was common in ANE temple practice — Mesopotamian temples prepared elaborate daily meals for their gods. Israel's law uses the language while implicitly denying its literal application: YHWH does not eat in the way temples literally fed their statues. The lehem isheh formula is metaphorical divine acceptance language.
12: The goat version of the fellowship offering — the simplest of the three, requiring no breed-specific fat tail rule. The presentation before YHWH is the same: slaughter at the sanctuary entrance, blood-sprinkling, fat burning.
13: The blood-sprinkling against the altar sides is consistent for goat fellowship offerings. The precision of the ritual — same blood manipulation regardless of animal — ensures that the theological content (blood at the altar = life offered before YHWH) is not diluted by variations in the offering animal.
14: YHWH's fat portions from the goat offering mirror those of the cattle (no fat tail, unlike the sheep). The standardized description of fat portions (entrail-covering fat, both kidneys, liver lobe) creates a consistent set of YHWH's portions across species, with the sheep exception for the fat tail being clearly marked as a breed-specific addition.
15: The two kidneys and liver lobe from the goat — identical to the cattle and sheep requirements. The repetition across three animal categories underscores that these specific fat organs are consistently YHWH's portion in the fellowship offering. The repetition in Leviticus (which some readers find tedious) is a legal precision device: specifying the rule for each case separately prevents legal loopholes.
16: kol-ḥelev laYHWH: 'All fat belongs to YHWH.' The general rule underlying all the specific fat-portion instructions: the best and richest part of every animal belongs to YHWH. The fellowship offering makes this explicit in summary form. The practical implication for the ancient Israelite: even in a celebration meal that included the offerer, YHWH's portion was the richest internal fat. This is not merely symbolic — it structured the actual food distribution at every fellowship sacrifice.
17: ḥuqqat olam ledoroteikhem bekol-moshevoteikhem: 'A lasting ordinance for all your generations wherever you live: you must not eat any fat or any blood.' The double prohibition on fat and blood is the most universalized law in the sacrificial code — extending beyond the land to wherever Israel lives, suggesting applicability even in diaspora contexts. This universal extension indicates these prohibitions encode something about the nature of life and holiness that transcends geography, unlike many land-specific laws.
